title: Civil rights group wants AG to address LGBTQ-related bullying in school hockey
author: Abby Patkin
bullets: Lawyers for Civil Rights urges Attorney General Andrea Campbell to address LGBTQ-related bullying in school hockey programs.
The civil rights group reports rising incidents of homophobic comments and exclusion in school-affiliated hockey programs.
The group urges the Massachusetts Interscholastic Athletic Association to address and prevent identity-based harassment in sports.

title: Al Horford supports Luke Kornet’s stance on Hawks’ ‘Magic City’ promotion
author: Khari A. Thompson
bullets: Luke Kornet criticized the Atlanta Hawks' promotion with strip club Magic City, urging reconsideration in an open letter.
Al Horford showed support for Kornet's stance by sharing his letter, despite his past with the Hawks.
The promotion includes Magic City wings and merchandise, sparking debate within NBA community about its appropriateness.

title: Patriots wide receiver Stefon Diggs scrubs Instagram account
author: Khari A. Thompson
bullets: Stefon Diggs removed all posts from his Instagram account but remains active via Instagram Stories.
Diggs is awaiting a pretrial hearing on April 1 for assault and strangulation charges.
Diggs's $6 million signing bonus becomes guaranteed on March 13 as the Patriots decide on his future.

title: Get one-way flights for as low as $79 during JetBlue’s spring sale
author: Kristi Palma
bullets: JetBlue launched a spring sale with one-way flights starting at $79, ending March 5 at 11:59 p.m. EST.
Discounted flights include trips from Logan Airport to Daytona Beach, Jacksonville, Nashville, and more, valid April 14 to June 10.
Travel restrictions include blackout dates from May 21-26, and sale fares apply to Tuesday and Wednesday travel.

title: All the new movies and TV shows streaming in March
author: Kevin Slane
bullets: Hulu adds three Oscar nominees: 'The Secret Agent', 'It Was Just an Accident', 'Sentimental Value'.
Netflix features 'Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man' and 'One Piece' Season 2 in March.
HBO Max debuts Steve Carell's 'Rooster' and Lisa Kudrow's 'The Comeback' this month.

title: Husband and wife charged with throwing rocks at passing cars in Southwick
author: Abby Patkin
bullets: Celestine Robinson and David Ducharme face multiple felony charges for allegedly throwing rocks at cars in Southwick, Massachusetts.
Robinson was charged with drunk driving, while Ducharme allegedly denied involvement in the rock-throwing incidents.
Robinson's bail was set at $1,500; Ducharme is held without bail due to his criminal history and other charges.

title: With deadline looming, still no word on security funding for World Cup in Foxborough
author: Molly Farrar
bullets: Foxborough awaits decision on $7.8 million security funding for World Cup at Gillette Stadium, deadline approaches March 17.
Town officials may block World Cup matches if organizers do not prepay security costs; funding responsibility remains unclear.
Massachusetts eligible for over $46 million in federal security funds; delays from Homeland Security and FEMA persist.

title: Lamplighter Brewing Co. workers vote to join union
author: Katelyn Umholtz
bullets: Lamplighter Brewing Co. staff in Cambridge voted to join United Food and Commercial Workers Local 1445 union.
Workers aim to negotiate their first contract, focusing on wages, benefits, health care, and workplace safety.
The unionization follows a wave of similar movements in the restaurant and bar industry over recent years.

title: Pedestrian hospitalized after being struck by MBTA bus in Norwood
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: A 35-year-old woman was struck by an MBTA bus on Washington Street in Norwood on Monday.
The woman was crossing outside a marked crosswalk and is in stable condition at a local hospital.
MBTA Transit Police are investigating the circumstances of the accident, hoping for her complete recovery.

title: A Mass. town voted down a new fire station. The old one just got shut down after the blizzard.
author: Abby Patkin
bullets: Westwood Fire Station 1 closed after blizzard damage made it unsafe for fire vehicles, prompting operations to move.
Voters rejected funding a new $38.1 million fire station twice, despite structural and safety problems at the existing station.
Temporary solutions include using other stations, while feasibility studies are planned for potential new sites for Fire Station 1.
